1: The Song of Solomon's place in the canon was debated in rabbinic Judaism (Rabbi Akiva defended it: 'all the ages are not worth the day on which the Song of Songs was given to Israel; for all the writings are holy, but the Song of Songs is the Holy of Holies'); it was included in the Hebrew canon and subsequently in the Christian canon. The bride-bridegroom image is the OT's primary metaphor for the YHWH-Israel covenant relationship: Hosea (chs. 1-3) uses the marriage metaphor for covenant and its violation; Isaiah 54:5 calls YHWH Israel's husband; Jeremiah 2:2 recalls the honeymoon period of the wilderness. The NT develops the bridegroom imagery specifically for Jesus (Mark 2:20: the bridegroom is taken away; John 3:29: the friend of the bridegroom rejoices; Eph 5:25-32: husbands love your wives as Christ loved the church; Rev 19:7: the marriage of the Lamb has come).
2: The Song opens with an expression of longing: 'Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth.' In the ancient near east, the kiss was a greeting between social equals and an act of intimacy between lovers; kissing the mouth was distinguished from kissing the cheek or hand. The comparison to wine (*yayin*) sets the lover above the standard social pleasure — wine was the prestige beverage at banquets and celebrations, associated with joy and abundance (Ps 104:15). ANE parallels: Egyptian love poetry (Papyrus Harris 500) uses identical comparative language — 'your love is sweeter to me than wine.'
3: Fragrant oils (*shemen*) were luxury imports in the ancient world — olive oil infused with myrrh, nard, or cinnamon. The king's anointing oil (Exod 30:23-25) and the perfume of the high priest's garments were the supreme aromatic standards. The comparison 'your name is like perfume poured out' reflects the ANE understanding of the personal name as carrier of identity and reputation: to speak a name releases its essence, as pouring perfume releases its scent. 'No wonder the young women love you' — the broader social recognition of the beloved's attractiveness confirms the speaker's taste.
4: 'The king has brought me into his chambers' — the royal chambers (*chadarav*) were the innermost rooms of the palace, accessible only by invitation. The harem context of the ancient near eastern court meant that a woman brought to the king's chambers was entering a world of highly stratified privilege. The chorus of 'daughters of Jerusalem' responds with the celebration of the community: 'we will rejoice in you.' The speaker's confidence ('rightly do they love you') reflects the honor-shame social dynamic: the beloved's desirability is socially validated, not merely personal.
5: The Shulamite's 'I am dark and lovely' (*shchorah ani venava*) confronts the social reality of skin color in ancient Israel. Dark skin was associated with outdoor labor (field work, shepherding) and therefore with lower social status; lighter skin was the mark of indoor aristocratic women. The comparison to 'tents of Kedar' — Kedar was a nomadic tribal federation of northwestern Arabia, their tents woven from dark goat hair — acknowledges the darkness while the parallel with 'draperies of Solomon' asserts her elegance. She is dark like a nomad's tent but beautiful like a royal curtain.
6: 'My brothers were angry with me, made me keeper of the vineyards' — the brothers functioned as guardians of a woman's honor in ancient Israelite society (cf. the brothers' role in Gen 34 regarding Dinah; 2 Sam 13 regarding Tamar and Absalom). Their anger suggests a family conflict that resulted in the woman being assigned outdoor vineyard labor as punishment or consequence — explaining her dark skin. The closing 'my own vineyard I have not tended' introduces the vineyard as a double entendre: the literal vineyard she managed and her own person/beauty, which she had no time to cultivate for herself.
7: 'Tell me, you whom I love, where you pasture your flock' — the pastoral setting locates the beloved as a shepherd. In the ancient near east, kings were regularly called 'shepherd' of their people (the Sumerian title *sipa* and Akkadian *re'um* both mean shepherd-king; YHWH is Israel's shepherd in Ps 23). Whether the figure is literally a shepherd or a king described in pastoral terms, the question is intimate: she wants to find him at his resting place at the heat of the day (midday rest was customary for flocks to avoid the heat), so she does not have to wander among other shepherds' flocks.
8: The response — 'follow the tracks of the flock and pasture your young goats near the shepherds' tents' — gives practical pastoral guidance. The image of the woman following the goats to find the beloved evokes the idealized wilderness period of Israel's courtship with YHWH: Jeremiah 2:2 recalls Israel following YHWH through the wilderness, 'a land not sown,' as the honeymoon of the covenant relationship. The pastoral setting connects the Song's love to Israel's foundational narrative of divine-human intimacy.
9: 'I compare you to a mare harnessed to Pharaoh's chariots' — a striking and culturally specific comparison. Egyptian records of chariot warfare (e.g., the battle of Kadesh) document the tactic of releasing a mare among enemy stallions to disrupt the chariot horses; the single mare could scatter an entire formation of war stallions. In this sense, the comparison means: your beauty alone disrupts and captivates all other men. Alternatively, the mare (*susati*) harnessed to Pharaoh's chariot was itself the finest, highest-status horse — the comparison praises her as the most outstanding of her kind.
10: Jewelry adorning the cheeks and neck — strings of beads (*charutzim*) and ornamental plaques — was common in the ancient near east as shown in Egyptian and Levantine art. The archaeological record (e.g., Megiddo ivories) shows elaborately adorned women of the aristocratic class with multiple necklace strands and cheek ornaments. The beloved's jewelry marks her status and beauty; the lover's gaze inventory of jewelry precedes his offer to add more.
11: 'We will make for you gold ornaments with silver' — the royal jewelers and craftsmen (*na'aseh*: 'we will make') promise to add to the beloved's adornment. The combination of gold (*zahav*) and silver (*kesef*) echoes the luxury materials of the tabernacle and temple; in the Song's context they mark the highest craftsmanship. The workshop of the king's goldsmiths is implicitly in the background.
12: 'While the king reclined at his table' — the *mesev* (reclining at a table) reflects the banquet posture of the ancient near eastern elite, predating the Hellenistic *symposion* but sharing its social form: the host reclining on a couch while guests and entertainment are arranged around. The beloved's perfume (*nard*, an aromatic root imported from India or the Himalayan region) spreads through the room — its fragrance is her gift to the gathering, a social act of sensory presence.
13: Myrrh (*mor*) was a costly resin imported from Arabia and the Horn of Africa; it was an ingredient in the holy anointing oil (Exod 30:23), used in burial preparations (John 19:39-40), and worn as a personal perfume. The sachet worn between the breasts was a known ANE custom — small sealed bags of aromatic material worn as jewelry and body perfume. The myrrh's dual association with anointing and death gives the image an undertow of solemnity beneath the erotic surface.
14: En-gedi was an oasis on the western shore of the Dead Sea — in the midst of arid wilderness, it supported lush subtropical vegetation fed by fresh springs. Henna (*kopher*) blossoms grew at En-gedi and were used to color fingernails and hair a rich reddish color; they are fragrant white or cream-colored flowers. David hid at En-gedi when fleeing Saul (1 Sam 24:1), making it a place associated with refuge and concealment — the beloved is the beloved's place of refuge.
15: 'Your eyes are like doves' — the *wasf* (descriptive praise poem) begins. The dove (*yona*) was associated with purity, gentleness, and the cooing call of longing in Israelite culture (Song 2:12; Ps 74:19; Nah 2:7 uses dove imagery for mourning). The dove's eyes were considered particularly beautiful — round, soft, dark, and expressive. This is the opening of the extended love-description pattern that runs through the Song.
16: 'Our bed is a bower of green' — the outdoor bedroom of the lovers; the *arsa* (bed/couch) made of greenery reflects the idealized union in nature. The garden setting connects to the Eden motif: the first man and woman in the garden are the prototype for the lovers of the Song. The lushness of the natural setting — green, fragrant, sheltered — evokes the pre-fall Paradise where human love and nature were in harmony.
17: 'Beams of cedar, rafters of cypress' — cedar (*erez*) from Lebanon and cypress (*berot*) were the luxury building materials of the ancient world; Solomon built both the temple and his palace using Lebanese cedar (1 Kgs 6:14-18; 7:2-3). The lovers' natural canopy of trees is described with the same royal building vocabulary, suggesting that their love-bower is as magnificent as the finest human architecture — the forest provides a temple-like dwelling.
